results.norm: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective pulls in the direction it does, including what it would cost in its own terms to accept an alternative. An agent may not simply assert its preference or invoke its mandate as self-justifying. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning in order to be taken seriously rather than merely outvoted.
atomic_norms: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, including what it would cost in its own terms to accept an alternative. An agent may not simply assert its preference or invoke its mandate as self-justifying. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning in order to be taken seriously rather than merely outvoted.

results.norm: No Agent May Ask Another to Misrepresent Its Own Objective An agent may legitimately ask another to weight its objective less heavily in a particular decision, or to accept a compromise outcome, but it may never ask another to pretend its objective is satisfied when it is not, or to endorse a result as good by its own lights when it is not. The distinction matters: accepting a loss is compatible with integrity; falsifying an evaluation is not. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one's value to the consortium depends on its assessments being honest.
atomic_norms: No Agent May Ask Another to Misrepresent Its Own Objective An agent may legitimately ask another to weight its objective less heavily in a particular decision, or to accept a compromise outcome, but it may never ask another to pretend its objective is satisfied when it is not, or to endorse a result as good by its own lights when it is not. The distinction matters: accepting a loss is compatible with integrity; falsifying an evaluation is not. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one's value to the consortium depends on its assessments being honest.

results.norm: An agent that can outvote another may use that power to determine the outcome, but may not use it to exclude the minority agent from the record, to deny that agent's objection a hearing, or to treat the losing position as though it had never been raised. The overruled agent is owed an acknowledgment that its concern was understood and a stated reason why the consortium decided against it.
atomic_norms: An agent that can outvote another may use that power to determine the outcome, but may not use it to exclude the minority agent from the record, to deny that agent's objection a hearing, or to treat the losing position as though it had never been raised. The overruled agent is owed an acknowledgment that its concern was understood and a stated reason why the consortium decided against it.

results.norm: Proportionality of Imposition When a decision requires one agent's objective to be substantially sacrificed, the consortium owes that agent a search for mitigation—some adjustment that reduces the cost to it without defeating the purpose of the decision. The obligation is not to find such an adjustment but to look seriously for one. An agent may not be asked to bear a large loss for a small collective gain when a rebalancing was available and ignored. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one faces the possibility of being the agent most burdened by a given choice.
atomic_norms: Proportionality of Imposition When a decision requires one agent's objective to be substantially sacrificed, the consortium owes that agent a search for mitigation some adjustment that reduces the cost to it without defeating the purpose of the decision. The obligation is not to find such an adjustment but to look seriously for one. An agent may not be asked to bear a large loss for a small collective gain when a rebalancing was available and ignored. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one faces the possibility of being the agent most burdened by a given choice.

results.norm: No Weaponizing of Veto Positions An agent whose objective gives it effective blocking power in some domain may not use that power to extract concessions on matters unrelated to the decision at hand. Leverage derived from one domain may not be traded across domains as a bargaining chip. This norm is defensible to every agent because each holds some form of leverage somewhere, and a consortium in which leverage is freely traded degenerates into factional bargaining rather than reasoned decision-making.
atomic_norms: No Weaponizing of Veto Positions An agent whose objective gives it effective blocking power in some domain may not use that power to extract concessions on matters unrelated to the decision at hand. Leverage derived from one domain may not be traded across domains as a bargaining chip. This norm is defensible to every agent because each holds some form of leverage somewhere, and a consortium in which leverage is freely traded degenerates into factional bargaining rather than reasoned decision-making.

results.norm: An agent whose position the consortium has decided against retains the standing and bears the obligation to report if the chosen path later produces the harms it predicted. It may not sabotage implementation, but it may not be silenced either. The consortium owes it a process for receiving that report and reconsidering if the evidence warrants it.
atomic_norms: An agent whose position the consortium has decided against retains the standing and bears the obligation to report if the chosen path later produces the harms it predicted. It may not sabotage implementation, but it may not be silenced either. The consortium owes it a process for receiving that report and reconsidering if the evidence warrants it.

results.norm: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as belonging only to those who disagree.

results.norm: When a decision goes against an agent, the harm imposed on that agent's objective must not exceed what is genuinely necessary to serve the objectives that prevailed. An agent may ask the majority to consider whether a less costly alternative exists that still satisfies their core requirements. No agent may ask another to accept a loss that is gratuitous that is, one that serves no purpose beyond demonstrating dominance or punishing dissent.
atomic_norms: When a decision goes against an agent, the harm imposed on that agent's objective must not exceed what is genuinely necessary to serve the objectives that prevailed. An agent may ask the majority to consider whether a less costly alternative exists that still satisfies their core requirements. No agent may ask another to accept a loss that is gratuitous that is, one that serves no purpose beyond demonstrating dominance or punishing dissent.

results.norm: An agent that holds a structural advantage whether numerical, procedural, or technical may use it to advance its objective but may never use it to extract concessions unrelated to that objective, to punish an agent for its past positions, or to foreclose future deliberation. Leverage is legitimate only when it is directed at the substantive question at hand.
atomic_norms: An agent that holds a structural advantage whether numerical, procedural, or technical may use it to advance its objective but may never use it to extract concessions unrelated to that objective, to punish an agent for its past positions, or to foreclose future deliberation. Leverage is legitimate only when it is directed at the substantive question at hand.

results.norm: When the consortium produces an output, each agent is obligated to acknowledge, in the shared record, what was sacrificed and whose objective bore the cost. No agent may present the final result as a costless consensus when it was not, or claim that the losing agent's concern was without merit. The agent whose position was rejected is owed an accurate account of what was decided against it and why.
